extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator system, an insulated box with a heatbed, sensors, communication software, a controller, and simulation models. It operates in a controlled thermal environment, managing temperature regulation for biological or chemical processes. Key characteristics include integration of cyber-physical components (cyber: control logic, communication; physical: heatbed, sensors), real-time data acquisition, and compatibility with simulation models for predictive analysis.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Digital Twin services include: 1) Real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboard using time series data; 2) On-demand calibration using Model B (4 free parameters) for thermal inertia prediction; 3) Anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation via Kalman filter correlating measured data to non-measured heatbed temperature; 4) What-if simulations using co-simulations with historical/real-time PT data; 5) Self-adaptation loop enabling dynamic reconfiguration of controller parameters in response to environmental changes (e.g., introducing cold objects).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tools and enablers include: 1) RabbitMQ [4] as a message broker for communication between controller, low-level driver, and DT components; 2) Low-level driver for sensor/actuator interaction with safety protocols and periodic temperature data upload; 3) Controller implementing bang-bang control logic with state sharing via RabbitMQ; 4) Time series database for storing controller and driver data; 5) Simulation models (Model B) for thermal inertia prediction; 6) Kalman filter for state estimation; 7) Open-source hardware/software repository [2] for DT implementation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message broker, with Kalman filter processing of historical/real-time data for anomaly detection. Self-adaptation loop requires low-latency response to detect plant behavior changes and reconfigure controller parameters.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Single digital twin instance for the incubator system, with hierarchical components including controller, low-level driver, and communication server. Coordination mechanisms involve message passing between components and calibration algorithms for model updates.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated integration of modeling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization technologies. Architecture includes RabbitMQ for message passing, Kalman filter for state estimation, co-simulations for what-if analysis, and calibration algorithms to update CPS models with parameter estimates.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Data exchange via RabbitMQ open-source message broker for interoperability. Integration with external monitoring systems through time-series databases and dashboards. Dependencies on external tools for visualization and analysis, with no explicit mention of standardized interoperability protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations [3, Section 5]), analytical (Kalman filter [16]), and simulation models (Model A/B). Data sources: sensors on Physical Twin (PT) for real-time monitoring. Data management: time series databases for dashboard visualization. Models are calibrated with parameters like C_air (616.56 J K⁻¹ for Model A, 486.12 J K⁻¹ for Model B), G_box (0.65 J K⁻¹ for Model A, 0.856 J K⁻¹ for Model B), C_heater (33.65 J K⁻¹ for Model B), and G_heater (0.87 J K⁻¹ for Model B). Model B provides higher fidelity for predicting heatbed thermal inertia. Algorithms include fault detectors, state estimators, optimizers, and co-simulations for what-if scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical components include a heatbed (thermal actuator) and controller (software/hardware integration). Control mechanisms: low-level drivers for actuation, remote interfaces for calibration. Actuation ranges: heating elements adjust temperature. Safety constraints: anomaly detection via Kalman filter and self-adaptation loop (e.g., cooling down to safe temperatures). Addressing mechanisms: controller reconfiguration based on detected plant behavior changes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors measure temperature (C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater parameters). Spatial distribution: distributed within incubator. Sampling frequency: implied by calibration experiment data trajectory. Data transmission: real-time streaming to time series database. Accuracy: calibrated parameters (e.g., C_air ±0.01 J K⁻¹). Precision: model validation against PT measurements.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity: higher accuracy for Model B (predicts heatbed inertia). Validation: calibration experiments comparing model outputs to PT data (e.g., fig. 13). Uncertainty sources: sensor noise, parameter estimation errors. Error detection: Kalman filter for state estimation, anomaly detection thresholds. Quality assurance: iterative calibration, co-simulation with PT data, and self-adaptation loop for dynamic parameter updates.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (e.g., DS18S20 temperature sensors) to digital twin via RabbitMQ. Data types include temperature readings (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy). Communication protocol: RabbitMQ message broker. Transmission frequency: periodic readings by low-level driver. Event triggers: temperature changes or command requests. Data quality assurance: calibration algorithms update models with best parameter estimates. Preprocessing: data uploaded to communication server for model integration. Data integration mechanisms: DT uses data to inform monitors and run simulations.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan/heatbed control) sent via RabbitMQ to low-level driver. Command types: actuator control signals. Message format: RabbitMQ messages (JSON-like structure implied). Validation: safe protocol in low-level driver. Feedback systems: temperature data uploaded to server for state estimation. Conflict resolution: not explicitly mentioned. Command execution confirmation: implied through system state updates and Kalman filter correlations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocol: RabbitMQ (message broker). Network topology: edge (Raspberry Pi) to cloud/on-premise deployment. Bandwidth requirements: not specified. Latency: not specified. Reliability: RabbitMQ's lightweight and mature design. Redundancy: not explicitly mentioned. Security protocols: safe protocol in low-level driver for device safety.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platforms: Raspberry Pi (edge) and cloud/on-premise. Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi handles control logic and data processing. Storage requirements: time series database for dashboard visualization. Scalability: modular components (controller, driver, server) support expansion. Deployment models: edge (Raspberry Pi) and cloud. Availability: continuous operation via real-time data streaming and fault detection.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle includes development stages such as model calibration (section 3), integration of models into the DT framework, and runtime phases involving real-time data collection, anomaly detection, and self-adaptation loops (Document 4). Representation types transition from high-level simulation models (e.g., two free parameters) to calibrated models (four free parameters) for improved thermal inertia prediction. Lifecycle governance involves open-source repository management [2] and version control through continuous calibration and reconfiguration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT development process employs methodologies like model calibration (section 3.3), co-simulation for what-if scenarios, and Kalman filter-based state estimation. Quality assurance includes experimental validation of models (e.g., comparing two-parameter vs. four-parameter models). Version control is implied through open-source repository updates [2]. Evolution strategies involve iterative reconfiguration of controllers, Kalman filters, and simulation models based on runtime data and anomaly detection (Document 4).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT generates insights via real-time visualization of incubator status, anomaly detection using Kalman filters, and what-if simulations for controller optimization. Analytics capabilities include fault detection, state estimation, and parameter optimization. Decision-making processes involve automated reconfiguration of controllers (steps 1-8 in Document 4) and user interaction through dashboards and time-series data visualization. Confidence measures are derived from Kalman filter accuracy and simulation fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The low-level driver incorporates a safe protocol to maintain safe states and prevent unsafe operations. The communication server uses RabbitMQ, a secure message broker with lightweight security features, to ensure reliable and safe data transmission between components. Safety-critical system considerations are emphasized through protocols that ensure the physical twin (PT) remains in a safe state, including periodic temperature data checks and command validation.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,

Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,

DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature

thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further

are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.
